data.title: Sprinklr: Good Developments, Not Good Enough
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Sprinklr (NYSE:CXM) previously with a hold rating, as I did not expect valuation upside until CXM is able to deliver tangible results from the initiatives implemented by the new CEO. I remain hold rated. While there are some early signs of stabilization and better execution, the revenue growth and net retention trends still suggest that a full turnaround is going to take time.

1Q26 earnings show some signs of stability.

CXM's 1Q26 results showed some really good signs of a turnaround. As a recap, here are some key numbers from the results. Total revenue came in at $205.5 million, up 5% y/y, with subscription revenue growing 4% y/y to $184.1 million. While headline growth is still down a lot from the 13% since 1Q25, 1Q26 did mark the first quarter in a long while that y/y growth accelerated sequentially. Profitability is another area of highlight. Adj. operating income hit $36.7 million, which is an 18% margin, up sharply from 11% in 1Q25 and ~200 bps above guidance. All of this directly translated to solid free cash flow [FCF]. CXM delivered a record $80.7 million FCF, and that would have been closer to $92.5 million if not for restructuring-related cash outflows. Overall, this was a very decent and good print that shows CXM is executing well.

Transformation Efforts Show Early Promise

When I last looked at CXM, many of their transformation efforts were still at the planning stage. But based on the 1Q26 update, it feels like they're beginning to gain some traction. The CEO clearly sounded more confident on the 1Q26 call, pointing to progress across several key initiatives. One of the initiatives that has shown early promise is Project Bearhug. This initiative aims to deepen CXM's relationships with its top 500 customers (who contribute ~80% of revenue). So far, CXM has spoken to hundreds of these clients (about 200 as of the end of May), and notably, these efforts have translated into expanded use cases. This is very helpful to know because it shows that there are a bunch of existing clients that are still underserved, and all CXM needs to do is to approach them. If these efforts continue to translate into upsells and stickier relationships, it would really improve the growth momentum and ease churn.

We're encouraged by the early results and new expanded use cases as we help companies drive accelerated ROI across the Sprinklr platform. Company 1Q26 earnings

The other update I had that got me more positive on the transformation plan is regarding the SKU front. This area has also clearly progressed past the initial planning stage. In the call, management mentioned that the product pipeline is the healthiest it's been in 18 months across Social, Marketing, and Insights (core portfolio). Clearly, CXM has shifted its resources to focus on its core business, and this is great because this is where CXM thrives. Sales incentives have been realigned to support core growth, and there's a new initiative called "Project Tiger Shark" focused on accelerating product innovation across the core SKUs. It would be very interesting to see what the new products are that CXM is going to launch, but this shows that management is making a serious effort to double down on what works.

One area that I would particularly like to see more development is the AI side of things. CXM has something good going on here. Its new AI-native platform could become a meaningful growth driver, especially in service. So far, this AI platform is helping customers reach agent deflection rates between 30% and 80%, which is massive since implies the average employee productivity is now up by 1.6x (average 40% deflection rate). If CXM can push out more of such AI products that generate value for clients, it would go a long way to help accelerate revenue growth.

Lastly, I think a very important proof point that shows CXM is on the right path to turnaround is that it continues to land large enterprise wins. In 1Q26, they had 146 customers generating >$1 million in subscription revenue, up 6% y/y. Although this is not an explosive growth, it supports the idea that the platform still resonates with global brands. Some of the major brands highlighted include companies like Calvin Klein, LG Electronics, and Pepsi. More impressively, CXM closed a major deal with a Fortune 500 medical tech firm to consolidate global operations and another with a large UK retailer to revamp its contact center. These are not small deployments, so they do speak very well of CXM's ability to support complex use cases at scale.

Persistent Headwinds and Still Uncertain Turnaround Timeline

Despite the encouraging improvements, I am still concerned about the near-term growth outlook, and this is the same caution I raised in my prior note. Putting all the developments aside, in 1Q26, revenue grew just 5% y/y, with subscription revenue up 4% y/y. It is still very far from the past years' double-digit revenue growth. It also doesn't help to learn that management's FY26 guide calls for just 3% y/y subscription growth at the midpoint, and they even went one step ahead to say that any meaningful re-acceleration likely won't happen until late FY26 or FY27.

Retention and expansion are also still areas of concern. Net dollar retention [NDR] continues to erode-down to 102% for 1Q26, from 107% just two quarters ago. The implication here is that CXM needs to rely more on winning new customers to grow, and that is not going to be easy if NDR continues to decline. A lot of work needs to be done on this front. If revenue grows and NDR contracts, I don't think the market will view it favorably. Even though the number of >$1 million annual recurring revenue [ARR] customers grew 6% y/y, 1Q26 still saw a decline of two of such customers sequentially. This isn't a disaster, but when I add this to the list of concerns I have, it just makes it harder to turn bullish.

Valuation

Given the way things are, I don't think a near-term growth acceleration is likely, which means CXM is still very far away from achieving double-digit revenue growth like what peers are delivering - HubSpot (HUBS), Freshworks (FRSH), and Braze (BRZE). I remained with the view that so long as CXM growth doesn't accelerate, it will continue to trade at a discount to peers. How much discount is debatable, but given the stark difference in growth outlook-peers with ~16% average growth and CXM with low single digits (almost 4x delta)-50% difference in multiples is not aggressive.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for CXM. It is indeed making progress, with promising traction from key transformation initiatives. But growth remains slow, NDR is trending down, and the overall timeline for reacceleration stretches well into FY27. With revenue growth expected to remain at low single digits, I don't see a clear catalyst for multiple expansion in the near term.

data.title: The Gabelli Global Mini Mites Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

During the first quarter, the Gabelli Global Mini Mites Fund declined 6.05%, compared to a return of (3.91)% for the S&P Developed Small Cap Index.

The top contributors to performance in the quarter were Velan Inc. (VLNSF, 2.5% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) and Avio S.p.A. (1.9%) Velan Inc. is a manufacturer of highly engineered valves and was up 62% during 1Q25, following the announcement of the sale of its French subsidiary to Framatome for $208 million and the concurrent divestiture of its asbestos liabilities to Global Risk Capital. The French transaction closed on March 31, and we believe it paves the way for an outright sale of the remaining assets. Avio S.p.A., listed in Milan, Italy, is a manufacturer of space transport and tactical missiles propulsion systems. It gained 29% in 1Q25 on the heels of solid 2024 results and positive long-term outlook. The company recently signed €350 million in new contracts with the European Space Agency and €150 million with MBDA Italy for the supply of rocket motors for CAMM-ER missiles. The EU is targeting a common fund of €800 billion to rearm Europe, and Avio is set to benefit from it. The company is also setting up a U.S. subsidiary to explore opportunities in the U.S. Avio recently signed research and development contracts with Raytheon and with the U.S. Army Combat Capabilities Development Command Aviation & Missile Center. If successful, these partnerships will prove to be very lucrative for Avio.

The top detractors in the quarter were Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI, 0.2%) and Harvard Bioscience, Inc. (HBIO, 0.3%). Applied Optoelectronics is an integrated provider of fiber optic networking products. The company is pursuing multi-year DOCSIS 4.0 network upgrades in cable television infrastructure and 400G data center products. It formed a strategic partnership with Microsoft for the development of 400G products, including exposure to artificial intelligence infrastructure. It is planning to make a large capex investment of $150 million in 2025 to build manufacturing capacity. It reported December quarter sales in line with its guidance and its shares declined 58% on the back of a sell off in data center/Al exposed stocks. Nvidia's plans to scale Al architecture using CPO (co-packaged optics) may present long-term revenue headwinds as the industry transitions from transceivers to CPOs. The company issued 7.9 million warrant shares to Amazon (AMZN) in March 2025, of which 1.3 million vested immediately on issuance, and with the remainder exercisable at $23.69. Harvard Bioscience manufactures life science equipment to support research and drug discovery. Their products include spectrophotometers, amino acid analyzers, mesh multielectrode arrays (MEA), clamp amplifiers, implantable telemetry products, and mobile activity rack systems. Shares declined 73% during 1Q25 as the company raised going concern risks as it seeks to explore alternative sources of capital to refinance outstanding debt due by 30 June 2025.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Tredegar Corp. (TG) 7.1%
Intevac Inc. 3.2
Velan Inc. (OTCPK:VLNSF) 2.5
Ampco-Pittsburgh Corp. (AP) 2.1
Park-Ohio Holdings Corp. (PKOH) 2.0
LB Foster Co. (FSTR) 1.9
Myers Industries Inc. (MYE) 1.9
Avio S.p.A. 1.9
Village Super Market Inc. (VLGEA) 1.8
Clarus Corp. (CLAR) 1.8
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Gencor Industries Inc. (0.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (GENC - $12.16 - NYSE ) designs, builds and sells equipment and materials used for the production of asphalt and highway construction machines. Shares appreciated during the quarter as its fiscal second quarter results outpaced expectations; Gencor reported over 30% sales and earnings growth. Additionally, its backlog rose to over $50 million, a favorable indicator as it benefits from large scale highway and infrastructure projects associated with the 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act.

Lee Enterprises Inc. (1.5%) (LEE - $10.38 - NASDAQ) is a legacy newspaper business serving 72 mid-sized markets based in Davenport, lowa, that is transforming itself into a digital news and subscription business. Lee's shares initially began appreciating last September following the sale of publishing assets to the Hoffman Media Group and subsequent filing that the Hoffmann Trust initiated a 5% ownership position, which expanded to nearly 10% by mid-December 2024. More recently, on March 20th, David Hoffmann sent a letter to the company in an effort to open discussions regarding a potential acquisition of LEE (but it did not contain any information around a potential acquisition price/terms), and the company responded on March 24th with an offering to enter into a confidentiality agreement, which would allow Hoffman to make a more formal proposal including a price and ability to finance the transaction. Two days later, LEE's Board of Directors unanimously approved an extension of the company's shareholder rights plan for another year (until March 27, 2026). On the company's Q1 earnings call, management reiterated its 2025 outlook for digital growth of 7%-10% and low single digit EBITDA growth, supported by $40mm of annualized cost savings LEE expects to have executed by Q2.

STRATTEC Security Corp. (1.3%) (STRT - $39.46 - NASDAQ) designs and manufacturers access systems and security solutions such as key fobs and power lift gate systems for vehicles. Shares appreciated following the release of its June results, which reflected growth across new products and pricing that contributed to higher margins. In June, STRATTEC named a new CEO, Jennifer Slater, who is undergoing a comprehensive review of the business, which we expect to conclude with a new operating strategy and financial targets. To date, Ms. Slater has identified several areas for operational efficiency improvements that should help drive greater profitability and gross margin predictability for the company.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS: Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A)

Gabelli Global Mini Mites Fund QTR 1 Year 3 Year 5 Year Since Inception (10/01/18)
Class I (GGMMX) (B) (6.05)% 1.38% 6.82% 20.02% 7.69%
S&P Developed SmallCap Index (C) (3.91) (0.78) 1.84 13.54 4.99

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS
Total Net Assets: $12.4 Million
NAV (Class I): $10.41
Turnover: (A) 26%
Inception Date: 10/01/18
Gross/Net Expense Ratio: (B) 3.12%/0.90%
SHARE CLASS SYMBOL
Class AAA: MUTF:GAMNX
Class A: MUTF:GMNAX
Class I: GGMMX

Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

(A) For the twelve months ended December 31, 2024.

(B) As of the current prospectus dated April 29, 2024. Net expense ratio after reimbursement from the Adviser. Effective through April 30, 2025, unless terminated early by the Fund's Board of Directors.

(A) Other classes of shares are available, with different characteristics. For additional information please contact your financial advisor or call 800-GABELLI.

(B) Returns would have been lower had Gabelli Funds, LLC, (the "Adviser") not reimbursed certain expenses of the Fund. The Fund imposes a 2% redemption fee on shares sold or exchanged within seven days of purchase.

(C) The S&P Developed SmallCap Index is a float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the equity market performance of small-capitalization companies located in developed markets. The index is composed of companies within the bottom 15% of the cumulative market capitalization in developed markets. The index covers all publicly listed equities with float-adjusted market values of U.S. $100 million or more and annual dollar value traded of at least U.S. $50 million in all included countries. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Investing in foreign securities involves risks not ordinarily associated with investments in domestic issues, including currency fluctuation, economic, and political risks.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

Investing in micro capitalization stocks may involve greater risk than investing in small, medium and large capitalization stocks since they can be subject to more abrupt or erratic movements in price. Micro cap companies may be illiquid.

Sarah Donnelly, Mario J. Gabelli, CFA, Ashish Sinha, Hendi Susanto, Chong-Min Kang

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JAKKS Pacific: A Rare Positive Risk Premium
data.description:
Those who know me best know that I have a few obsessions. I like playing Dungeons & Dragons. I like painting. I like writing. I like the soap opera “The Young & The Restless.” All of these obsessions pale in comparison to my strong desire to make the lives of my readers as pleasant as possible. One of the ways I do this is by offering up a thesis statement at the beginning of each article. This benefits those readers who want a bit more than what they get from a title and bullet points, and less than they’d get from the full “Doyle article experience.” So, I’m going to be taking a relatively small position in this JAKKS Pacific (NASDAQ:JAKK) stock this afternoon (June 5th) for a few reasons. First, I like the financial performance, and the strength of the balance sheet. Although the dividend has been reintroduced only recently, I think it’s sustainable. Finally, the stock is attractively priced relative to the risk-free alternative, which makes it a bit of a standout at the moment. Thus ends the thesis statement portion of the article. If you read on, that’s on you.

Introduction of The Business

It's been written that the purpose of these articles on Seeking Alpha is to give investors ideas that they can then explore in greater depth, putting each investment idea in the context of their own circumstances. This is why I tend to focus on analysis that may not be too obvious. That written, I also want to let you know about the business in case you're unable to access the company's investor page, or latest slide deck. That's just one more of the value added services I want to offer my readers. You're welcome.

JAKKS is a leading designer, manufacturer, and marketer of toys and consumer products sold throughout the world. The company's seen very strong growth recently from Sonic the Hedgehog 3, Disney Moana 2, and DreamWorks Animation Dog Man among others.

One of the reasons the first quarter of this year was much better (less bad) than previous is because the company launched a host of new products that seem to be doing well. In particular, I'm impressed by the fact that the company's Dolls, Role Play/Dress Up business increased sales 37% from the year ago period. I also like the geographic diversification, as evidenced by the fact that North American sales were higher by 25%, and International sales were up 29% from the year ago period.

The company sells a wide range of entertainment-inspired products featuring premier licensed properties, including The Simpsons, Disney's Little Mermaid, and The Super Mario Brothers Movie properties. The following presents a more full view of the properties.

I hope this short introduction to the business doesn't interfere with my desire to save as much of your time as possible. I guess you'll be reading about the business eventually anyway, so it may as well be here.

Financial Snapshot

I think the financial history is reasonable enough. The company is subject to the business cycle, obviously, but it seems to be on an uptick. The reason I suggest this is the fact that revenue in the first quarter of 2025 was up by about 25.75%, and net loss has shrunk by about 83.6% from a loss of $14.5 million last year to $2.4 million in 2025. Additionally, the capital structure is reasonably strong, with total assets of $406 million to total liabilities of $170.8 million. Additionally, I think it’s relevant that $59 million of the $406 is cash, so there’s no problem with liquidity here in my view. Given the above, I’d be happy to buy the shares if the valuation is reasonable and if the dividend is sustainable.

Dividend Sustainability

As I just suggested above, the sustainability of the dividend is of critical importance here, and I want to focus on it because it’s got such a short pedigree in this case. At least one investor commented on my Mattel post that they prefer JAKK to Mattel because of the dividend. Thus, in the absence of the dividend, it’s reasonable to suppose that some investors would abandon the name, which would have obvious implications on price.

Although I love accrual accounting as much as it’s possible to love accrual accounting, I am of the view that when it comes to dividends and their sustainability, cash is king. So, I’m going to review JAKKS Pacific’s likely sources, and uses of cash. I’m in the mood to start by looking at sources, so I’m going to start looking at sources of cash. As of the latest 10-Q, the company has about $59.18 million in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet. Additionally, over the past three years, they’ve generated an average of $63.8 million in cash from operations. For those of you who may not have been victimized by a course or two in financial statement analysis, I’ll let you know that accounting standards “add back” to cash from operations the depreciation expense. While this may be technically correct when we’re looking only at cash, I have a problem with it because depreciation is a real thing. It imposes a real economic cost, so I’m also going to calculate cash from operations, but with the depreciation add back stripped out. When I do this, my adjusted average cash from operations figure drops to $54 million.

At the same time, the company has invested an average of about $10.7 million in the business over the past three years. So far, I’m pretty optimistic.

In terms of obligations, I’ve plucked the following from page 34 of the latest 10-K for your enjoyment and edification. In terms of financial obligations, it seems that 2025 is a pretty "spicy meatball", as the young people say. The company is on the hook for $72.2 million this year, and $39 million next year, and $18 million in 2027. Please note, per the highlighted portion below, the one variable here is taxes. We need to always remember that all governments everywhere need to wet their beak. I mean, they need a taste because this business is done in their territory. It’s a matter of respect.

In spite of the risk of government representatives showing up in shiny suits , making offers that can't be refused, and talking about what a nice business JAKK Pacific is, and how much of a shame it’d be if something happened to it, I think the dividend is sustainable. If the valuation is reasonable, I’d be happy to buy at current prices.

The Stock v Treasury Note

Some people would fear coming off as repetitive. If you read my stuff regularly, you know that this is not a fear that I suffer from. Given that, I’m going to write it one more time. In the world of investing, everything’s relative. Also, we investors are always looking for the greatest risk-adjusted returns. We're not looking for "returns." We're looking for "risk-adjusted returns." One of the implications of this point is that a stock, which is generally riskier than the 10 Year Treasury Note, must offer a better return than debt backed by the full faith and credit of Uncle Sam. Put another way, if a stock can’t generate the same level of cash flow as the risk-free rate, why on Earth would you buy it?

In terms of the specifics of comparing a given stock to the Treasury Note, I ask, and then answer, the question “at what rate will the dividend need to grow/can the dividend fall over the next decade for stock investors to receive the same cash flows as Treasury Note holders?” Please note that I think they should receive more than Treasury holders, given the risks involved, but baby steps. At the moment, the yield on the 10-Year Treasury Note is about 4.392%, and the dividend yield on JAKK is about 4.67%. Per the table below, the dividend could fall at a CAGR of about 1.5% for the shareholder to receive the same cash flows as the Treasury Note holder over the next decade. In my view, the dividend is more likely to rise, so the risk premium is actually positive here. This fact makes JAKKS a relatively rare stock at the moment.

Given the above, I'll be taking a small (300 shares) speculative position in this stock, and will likely add as the year progresses assuming the bull thesis remains intact.

Risks To My Thesis

Painful experience has shown me that my certainty is sometimes not warranted. It's for that reason that I want to write about the risks associated with this strategy. A superficial analysis might suggest that companies like this might be harmed by a recession. It turns out, though, that toys are relatively recession-resistant. Given that, I think the biggest risk to this thesis is an uptick in interest rates from current levels. One of the reasons I think this investment is quite attractive is because it offers a sustainable yield that's superior to the 10-Year Treasury Note. If the risk-free rate exceeds the yield here, the bullish thesis is materially muted in my view.

Conclusion

To sum up, I'm of the view that this company is growing its cash flows at a decent clip, and is therefore financially strong, particularly the capital structure. The dividend is sustainable in my view, given the delta between cash from operations, and cash needed for investment and contractual obligations. That dividend offers a higher yield at the moment than the risk-free rate, which makes this a relatively rare investment indeed at the moment. My advice to investors is to buy a small position here, and add to that position as the bullish thesis is borne out. I would also pay attention to the term structure of interest rates. If rates start to rise materially, the relative value of this investment will fall in my view, and that would call for obvious action.

data.title: Tracking Carl Icahn's 13F Report - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Carl Icahn’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Icahn’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/15/2025. Please visit our Tracking Carl Icahn’s Portfolio series to get an idea of his investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund’s moves during Q4 2024.

The number of holdings in the 13F filing remained steady at 14. The top five holdings in the filing are Icahn Enterprises, CVR Energy, Southwest Gas Holdings, CVR Partners, and International Flavors & Fragrances.

Carl Icahn is best known for building sizable stakes in businesses and then pushing for changes to increase shareholder value. To learn more about his investing style and philosophy check out “King Icahn: The Biography of a Renegade Capitalist”.

Note 1: On March 28, 2025, Icahn disclosed holding 27.46M shares (8.44% of business) of Endeavor Group. The acquisition of Endeavor Group by private equity firm Silverlake for $27.50 cash closed soon after. The idea is to join a lawsuit that seeks to revalue the shares at a higher price (appraisal rights).

Note 2: Icahn's 13F cannot be considered a listing of his holdings. Carl Icahn is the majority owner of Icahn Enterprises (NASDAQ:IEP) which holds several of the businesses and the entities in turn hold IEP. His 13F is a consolidated report. IEP Investor Presentation (Slide 5) has details of the business structure.

Stake Increases:

CVR Energy Inc. (CVI): CVI is a top three ~18% of the 13F portfolio position first purchased in Q4 2011. The bulk of the current position (66.69M shares: ~66% of the whole business) was purchased through a $30 per share tender offer in H1 2012. Q3 2023 saw a ~6% trimming at an average price of ~$35.20 per share. The stock currently trades at $23.22. There was a minor ~3% stake increase this quarter.

Note 1: Two MLPs were carved out since the 2012 tender: CVR Refining the refining portion and CVR Partners (UAN) the nitrogen fertilizer unit. CVR Energy had majority ownership stake in both. In August 2015, CVR Partners agreed to merge with Rentech Nitrogen. Q2 2018 saw a tender offer whereby new CVR Energy shares were exchanged for CVRR at a valuation of $24.26 per common unit (0.6335 shares of CVI for one CVRR). ~22M new shares were issued.

Note 2: Their ~$30 implied cost-basis is vastly overstated due to carve-outs and special dividends paid out over the years.

CVR Partners LP (UAN): CVR Energy has a ~38% ownership stake in CVR Partners and it is listed separately in the 13F report as of Q1 2024. UAN currently trades at $82.19. There was a minor ~5% stake increase during the last quarter and a marginal increase this quarter.

JetBlue Airways (JBLU): The 2.16% of the portfolio stake in JBLU was established during Q1 2024 at prices between $4.70 and $7.45. There was a ~90% stake increase this quarter at prices between $4.72 and $8.31. The stock currently trades near the low end of those ranges at $4.88.

Illumina Inc. (ILMN): ILMN stake was established during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$194 and ~$233. The position was sold down by ~90% during the last quarter at prices between $128.98 and $155.15. It was rebuilt this quarter at prices between ~$78 and ~$153. The stock currently trades near the low end of those ranges at $82.25.

Stake Decreases:

Southwest Gas Holdings (SWX): SWX is a 7.22% of the portfolio position established over the two quarters through Q3 2021 at prices between ~$63 and ~$72. There was a ~75% stake increase during Q3 2022 through a tender offer at $82.50 per share. That was followed by a ~60% further increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$59 and ~$67. The last quarter saw a ~13% reduction at prices between $67.33 and $78.53. That was followed by a ~22% selling this quarter at prices between ~$68 and ~$79. The stock currently trades at $71.48.

Note 1: They have a ~10% ownership stake in the business.

Note 2: The position was made public in an October 2022 regulatory filing when they said they were opposed to Southwest’s acquisition of Questar Pipeline, Dominion Energy’s (D) transportation and storage business.

Kept Steady:

Icahn Enterprises (IEP): IEP position has seen consistent increases over the years. The position size increased from ~98M shares to ~102M shares in 2013, to ~109M shares in 2014, to ~117M shares in 2015, to ~130M shares in 2016, to ~158M shares in 2017, 175.4M in 2018, 197M in 2019, 222M shares in 2020, 257M shares in 2021, 300M shares in 2022, and 450M shares as of Q1 2025. The stock currently trades at $8.45. This is compared to ~$6 NAV per share as of Q1 2025.

Note 1: The stake increase over the years is primarily due to them taking dividend consideration in additional shares rather than cash. The dividend was cut by 50% to $1 per unit in August 2023. It was cut by another 50% to 50c per unit in November 2024.

Note 2: During May 2023, IEP stock price dropped sharply following a short report from Hindenburg Research.

International Flavors & Fragrances (IFF): IFF is now a 3.89% of the portfolio stake. A small position was established in Q1 2022 at prices between ~$116 and ~$150. Q1 2024 saw the stake built at prices between ~$78 and ~$86. The stock currently trades near the low end of those ranges at $76.46.

Bausch Health (BHC): The ~3% of the portfolio BHC position was purchased in Q4 2020 at prices between ~$15.30 and ~$21 and increased by ~575% in the next quarter at prices between ~$21.25 and ~$34.40. The stock currently trades well below the low end of those ranges at $4.63.

Note: They own 34.7M shares (~10% of the business). Two Icahn nominees are on the board.

Dana Inc. (DAN): The 2.54% DAN stake was established in Q4 2020 at prices between ~$13.25 and ~$20 and increased by ~200% in the next quarter at prices between ~$19 and ~$27. Q3 2021 also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between $20.90 and $24.60. The stock currently trades at $16.47.

Note: Icahn controls ~10% of Dana Inc.

American Electric Power (AEP): AEP is a small 1.76% stake established during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$71 and ~$84 and the stock currently trades well above that range at ~$102.

Caesars Entertainment (CZR): CZR is a 0.81% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between $31.85 and $43.98 and the stock currently trades well below that range at $25.63.

Note: It was reported in May that Ichan has established a stake in Caesar’s Entertainment (CZR) – he has no plans for activism this time around. From early 2019, he was involved in activism at Caesar’s. Details follow: They acquired ~66M shares of Caesars Entertainment (~10% of the business) in early 2019. This included ~5.8M shares in convertible bonds. Total outlay was $646M for a cost-basis of $9.78 per share. The position was since increased to ~120M shares of beneficial ownership (17.55% of business, majority through equity swaps and convertible bonds). Icahn pushed for a sale of the company and in April Anthony Rodio who previously ran Icahn’s Tropicana Entertainment was appointed CEO in a deal with the company. In June Eldorado Resorts (ERI) agreed to buy the company in a cash-and-stock deal ($8.40 cash and 0.0899 ERI shares for each share of old CZR held). The transaction closed in July 2020, and the combined business took the Caesar Entertainment name. Icahn exited the position soon after.

Sandridge Energy (SD): SD is a 0.73% of the portfolio activist stake established in Q4 2017 at prices between $16 and $21 and the stock is now at $10.38.

Note: Icahn has a ~13.5% ownership stake in the business. He lobbied the board and succeeded in ending the Bonanza Creek acquisition on December 28, 2017. In June 2018, Icahn gained control of Sandridge’s board by winning a proxy battle.

Bausch + Lomb Corp (BLCO): BLCO is a spinoff from Bausch Health that started trading in May 2022 at ~$18 per share. The stock currently trades at $11.61. In June 2022, two nominees of Icahn joined the board.

Centuri Holdings (CTRI): The small 0.54% CTRI stake was established during Q2 2024 at $21 per share. CTRI is a carve-out from Southwest Gas that started trading in April. Icahn acquired the shares through a concurrent private placement that closed immediately following the IPO. Shares started trading at ~$24 and is currently at $22.64. Q3 2024 saw a ~40% stake increase at prices between $14.92 and $21.45 while during the last quarter there was a ~30% reduction at prices between $15.51 and $21.75.

Note: Icahn also owns ~90% of Viskase Companies (OTCPK:VKSC). He is also known to have a position in Fannie/Freddie (OTCQB:FNMA) (OTCQB:FMCC).

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Icahn’s 13F stock holdings in Q1 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Carl Icahn’s 13F filings for Q4 2024 and Q1 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EchoStar Corporation Has Entered The End Game (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
To be frank with you, things have not been going very well for shareholders of EchoStar Corporation (NASDAQ:SATS) recently. In fact, they haven't been going well for quite some time. Since I last reaffirmed the company as a Sell candidate back in October of 2024, shares have fallen by 20.7%. That plunge is a world apart from the 4.2% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) enjoyed over the same window of time. This decline occurred even after factoring in the 17.5% rise that shares experienced on June 5th. That followed an 11.3% drop that the stock experienced on June 3rd after management announced that it was withholding payment on interest for some of its debt because of uncertainty caused by a regulatory issue that could affect its spectrum.

This alone poses a threat for shareholders. But in all honesty, even if this was not an immediate problem, it looks as though the company's days are numbered. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all declining materially. Yes, shares of the business look attractive in some respects. But when you consider that almost all parts of the company are continuing to worsen across the board, especially the largest part that is its Pay-TV segment, I honestly see no redeeming qualities here. In light of the challenges that the company has faced, I believe that downgrading it from a Sell to a Strong Sell is the appropriate choice right now.

A multitude of problems

Operationally speaking, EchoStar is not doing well right now. In the chart above, as an example, you can see financial results for the last three completed fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see results for the first quarter of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024.

What we see here is a consistent decline almost across the board. Admittedly, net profits have been a bit inconsistent. But when it comes to revenue and every measure of cash flow that the company has, the situation has only worsened.

This is not the picture of a company that is doing well. I have, in the past, bought into turn around prospects and achieved significant success in the process. But in those cases, there are certain attributes that, here, are missing. For instance, in those other opportunities, there is often some big part of the company that is actually doing well. That is not the case here. Do you see what I mean?

We should probably dig into each of the three operating segments that the business has.

The first and largest of these is the Pay-TV segment. This is the part of the company that provides pay TV services under the DISH and SLING brands. It also focuses to some extent on the designing, developing, and distributing of receiver systems, plus it provides digital broadcast operations like satellite uplinking and downlinking, transmission, and other services, to third-party pay TV providers. This is largely focused around the DISH brand, while the SLING brand provides multi-channel, live-linear, and on-demand streaming over-the-top internet based video programming services.

From 2022 through 2024, revenue for this segment contracted from $12.51 billion to $10.69 billion. This decline in sales brought with it lower profits and cash flows as well. As an example, operating income contracted from $2.93 billion to $2.65 billion, while OIBDA fell from $3.36 billion to $2.99 billion. This weakening for the segment can be attributed to the fact that subscriber count for the company has fallen from 9.75 million to 7.78 million. This was more than enough to offset the rise in monthly ARPU from $101.20 to $108.90.

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the picture worsened also. Revenue of $2.54 billion fell short of the $2.73 billion generated the previous year. Thanks to this, operating income contracted from $670.1 million to $653.4 million while OIBDA dropped from $755.5 million to $729.9 million. A decline in the number of subscribers from 8.18 million to 7.40 million caused this drama and had a significantly more negative impact than the positive impact from a rise in monthly ARPU from $107.38 to $110.64.

This has not been the only part of the company that has suffered. From 2022 through 2024, the firm’s Wireless segment reported a decline in revenue from $4.18 billion to $3.61 billion. This drop has brought with it a worsening in the firm's operating income from negative $888.2 million to negative $2.83 billion. Meanwhile, its OIBDA worsened from negative $599.6 million to negative $1.70 billion. During this time, the company saw a drop in the number of wireless subscribers from 7.98 million to 7 million. It also saw a drop in monthly ARPU from $37.72 to $36.57.

For those not familiar with the company, it is worth mentioning that the Wireless segment for the business focuses on providing wireless communication services and products to its customers, with the big push for the company being its 5G voice over new radio (or VoNR) offering and its 5G broadband service. These services are provided under its Boost Mobile and Gen Mobile brands.

For the first quarter of 2025, revenue was $972.8 million. That was actually up from the $914 million reported the previous year and was driven by a rise in monthly ARPU from $36.69 to $37.89. However, the number of subscribers dropped year over year from 7.30 million to 7.15 million. There is some good news here. The number of subscribers that the company had under this segment was up about 150,000 on a net basis compared to the previous quarter. Management attributed that to lower rates of churn and a rise in government subsidized subscribers. Most of the revenue increase, however, came from additional equipment sales and other revenue. This was because of a rise in the number of units shipped and wireless devices, not to mention a growth in revenue per unit shipped caused by changes in unit mix.

This can all be attributed to the quarter over quarter rise in subscriber count. But this improvement in revenue did not stop the company from reporting a worsening in profitability, with its operating loss growing from $645.2 million to $722.3 million, and it's OIBDA worsening from negative $363.5 million to negative $415.1 million.

There is also the Broadband and Satellite Services segment. Through this unit, the company provides broadband services to its customers, as well as certain satellite related services that helped to facilitate communications. From 2022 through 2024, revenue for this segment contracted from $2 billion to $1.58 billion. That caused a worsening in its operating income from $181.6 million to negative $117.9 million. At the same time, it saw a reduction in OIBDA from $644.4 million to $341.9 million. All of this pain was attributable to a drop in the number of subscribers from 1.23 million to 883,000.

Continued pain came in the first quarter of 2025, with revenue of $370.7 million falling short of the $382.6 million reported one year ago. We did see profitability metrics improve, with the firm's operating loss narrowing from $39.6 million to $19.2 million, and with its OIBDA improving from $79.3 million to $85.7 million. But with the number of subscribers contracting from 978,000 last year to 853,000 this year, the overall trend here is clear.

In the past, there was some evidence that a turnaround for the company might be possible. Last October, for instance, I wrote about the firm's decision to sell its video distribution business, which included DISH TV and Sling TV in exchange for $1. While this sounds absurd, the transaction also brought with it a meaningful reduction in debt. The buyer, DIRECTV had agreed to assume all of the debt of the business. And when you factor in other changes that would have been brought to the table, the end result would have been a $7 billion reduction in net debt. It would have also relieved the company of another $11.6 billion in future obligations that would have proven helpful for the company. But in late November of last year, the buyer terminated the purchase agreement because certain exchange offers that the company deemed necessary in order for the deal to go through ended up not being consummated.

Investors who are bullish about the company might also point out that there is significant potential in the 5G side of things. The firm has, in recent years, invested around $30 billion into wireless spectrum. And it has been focusing on growing its 5G subscriber base. The company had a strict timeline in which to achieve certain coverage obligations covering 80% of the U.S. population in each "Economic Area" defined by the FCC. That was supposed to be achieved at the end of December of last year.

And on January 10th, in a filing to the FCC, EchoStar certified that it had achieved 5G broadband service coverage for at least 85% of the population in each "Economic Area." According to management, it boasts 5G VoNR coverage of over 220 million Americans and 5G broadband service for more than 268 million. But even with that kind of coverage, the company is still losing ground fast, with revenue, profits, and cash flows, all contracting sharply. There is an opportunity for the company to transition more of its users from utilizing network services under agreements that it has with T-Mobile USA (TMUS) and AT&T (T), which could help it. But I don't see it making a great deal of difference.

But even to the extent that this might prove positive for shareholders, the firm is now facing additional challenges. Recently, news broke that the FCC is investigating whether EchoStar actually did hit the requirements to deploy 5G that it had been forced to meet. This could lead to significant risks for the company and has caused the firm, citing uncertainty for its business, to miss not one, but two, interest payments on some of its debts.

In a filing that came out on June 2nd, management said that they were holding off on paying $183 million in cash interest payments on some of their debts. This followed management's decision to delay payment on other debt, with that delayed payment amounting to interest of $326 million. These technically amount to defaults on their debt and start a 30-day grace period whereby the firm can either make the payments as it is contractually obligated to, or be pushed into bankruptcy.

While the firm could potentially receive a waiver on these payments, none of this changes the fact that the overall picture for the company is getting worse. In the chart above, you can see its net debt and its net leverage ratio from 2022 through 2024. You can also see estimates for 2025 if net debt remains unchanged from where it was in the first quarter of this year and if we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year when it comes to EBITDA.

In the chart below, you can see how the company is priced using these estimates and using historical results from 2024. The elevated EV to EBITDA multiple, which is far higher than the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple, illustrates why a multifactorial approach to valuing companies is important and it also demonstrates the precarious situation that the business is in. Even if shares were materially cheaper, I wouldn't say that anything less than a bearish assessment would be appropriate given the totality of the circumstances.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think that things are going to get worse for EchoStar Corporation from this point on. I honestly don't see any redeeming qualities in the business at this time. Fundamentally, the firm is a mess, and financial performance is probably going to continue to worsen. On top of this, there is now the prospect of bankruptcy if it does not start paying its interest as it is contractually obligated to do. At the end of the day, I think that these challenges necessitate a downgrade to a Strong Sell.

data.title: Broadcom: Strong Growth From This AI Winner
data.description:
Article Thesis

Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) reported its most recent earnings results on Thursday afternoon. While the company beat estimates on both lines while also providing appealing guidance numbers for the current quarter, AVGO shares do seem somewhat pricey, which is why I do not see Broadcom as a great investment at current prices.

Past Coverage

I have written about Broadcom here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently late last year, when I covered Broadcom's deal with Apple (AAPL). In that article, I was bearish on AAPL, which is down since then, while I was more optimistic, although not overly bullish, on Broadcom. Around half a year has passed since then, which was eventful for the company -- it reported earnings results twice, including today, and has seen its shares trade down to below $150 before shares soared to the $260s again.

With two quarters having passed since my last article on AVGO, and with the company reporting its most recent earnings results on Thursday, it is time for an update.

What Happened?

On Thursday afternoon, following the market's close, Broadcom reported its fiscal Q2 results. These were the headline numbers:

We see that Broadcom generated highly appealing revenue growth, which was not a surprise, of course, thanks to the company's exposure to macro tailwinds such as cloud computing and AI. Growth was marginally better than expected, at just above 20%. The company also beat the consensus earnings per share estimate slightly, but the market didn't like these results a lot -- at the time of writing, AVGO trades down by a couple of percentage points. Let's delve into the results.

Broadcom's Strong Q2 And Positive Outlook

Tech is a high-growth industry, but still, most tech companies do not grow by 20% per year. Of course, outliers such as Nvidia (NVDA) exist, but apart from the king of AI, major tech companies mostly generate lower growth compared to Broadcom. The Magnificent 7, for example, grew like this over the last year:

Nvidia aside, the Mag 7 companies mostly grew their revenues by 5% to 15% in the most recent quarter, with Tesla (TSLA) being an outlier to the downside due to its substantial revenue decline. Relative to these companies, Broadcom's growth looks quite compelling -- maybe one of the reasons why some believe that Broadcom, not Tesla, should be a member of the Mag 7 companies.

Broadcom's revenue growth was broad-based, with both business units delivering strong results, as we can see in the following table:

Semiconductor solutions, Broadcom's bigger unit, grew at a slightly slower but still appealing rate of 17% compared to one year earlier, while the somewhat smaller Infrastructure software business grew by a very nice 25% year-over-year. This unit, which saw a major boost from the VMware acquisition that closed in 2023, is gaining relevance for Broadcom. As its relative contribution to AVGO's overall revenues grows, Broadcom's overall revenue growth rate could be positively impacted. Broadcom's guidance for the current quarter, its fiscal Q3, implies revenue growth of ~20% as well, in line with the second quarter.

Management noted that Broadcom's AI revenue, which is not broken out into a distinct unit, grew by a very nice 44% compared to one year earlier, with AI revenue coming in at $4.4 billion for the quarter, or close to a third of AVGO's total revenues. This isn't surprising, of course, as the heavy investments in AI data centers by companies such as Meta Platforms (META) or Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) do not only drive demand for Nvidia's chips, but also for other AI hardware such as Broadcom's switches. With the recent news about the release of its new high-powered Tomahawk 6 switch just two days ago, Broadcom should be well-positioned to capture a significant portion of the growing data center hardware market in the future, too. I thus believe that AI will continue to remain a growth driver for Broadcom in the future.

Sales are important, but so are margins and profits. Here, things looked really good, at least when we focus on Broadcom's adjusted results. The company hit an adjusted EBITDA margin of 67%, which is pretty appealing, and since depreciation and amortization are relatively low while AVGO's tax rate is low as well, Broadcom's adjusted net margin came in at a very strong 52%. That compares very favorably to the net margins by other major tech companies, including the Magnificent 7 -- not even Nvidia's net margin was this high, as it came in at 45% during the most recent quarter.

Thanks to a combination of compelling revenue growth and a strong margin performance, Broadcom was able to grow its adjusted earnings per share by 44% during the second quarter, which was more than twice the company's revenue growth rate over the same period. Clearly, Broadcom has substantial pricing power, which is why its EBITDA margins are so high, while operating leverage allows the company to grow its operating and net margins as its business scales up.

When we look at GAAP results, things do not look quite as good, however. GAAP earnings are way lower than adjusted earnings -- GAAP earnings per share were $1.03 for Q2, which is not even two-thirds of the adjusted result of $1.59. It makes sense to adjust for some items that are not recurring, such as legal expenses or takeover closing costs. But other expenses are recurring and excluding them from adjusted results paints a potentially too-bright picture.

One of these recurring expenses that Broadcom excludes from its adjusted results is share-based compensation expenses. Broadcom is by far not the only company that excludes those, as many other companies do so as well -- but in AVGO's case, those are pretty meaningful, and due to the dilution that share issuance to management and employees causes, share-based compensation is an expense with a substantial "real-world" impact. Share-based compensation expenses totaled $1.8 billion in Q2, or close to 40% of the company's GAAP net profit of $5.0 billion -- while some may disagree, I believe that this is an expense that shouldn't be ignored.

The impact of Broadcom's hefty pace of share issuance as a form of compensation can be seen when we look at Broadcom's share count. During the second quarter, the share count averaged 4.826 billion shares, up half a percentage point over the last year -- despite Broadcom spending $4.2 billion on share repurchases during the second quarter alone, and a total of $9 billion over the last year. When a company spends close to a billion dollars on buybacks every month and its share count is still climbing, that shouldn't be ignored, I think.

Broadcom: Growth Appeal, But Pricy Valuation

Broadcom has seen its shares roughly double from the April lows, which naturally has made its shares a lot pricier than they were two months ago. Based on current earnings per share estimates for this year, Broadcom trades for 39x net profits. Analysts are using adjusted earnings per share estimates, which means that the GAAP earnings multiple would be considerably higher -- if the ratio of GAAP earnings to adjusted earnings remains stable throughout the remainder of the year, the GAAP earnings multiple would be in the 60x range.

The company's enterprise value to EBITDA ratio, which accounts for changes in debt usage and for cash held on the balance sheet, is quite high as well:

At 31x, the EV/EBITDA ratio is high in absolute terms, and it is high relative to where it was in the recent past -- which is not too surprising, as AVGO rallied by almost 100% over the last year alone.

I believe that Broadcom Inc. will continue to deliver appealing business growth in the foreseeable future, thanks to AI tailwinds and its strong Infrastructure software business. But I'm not willing to pay almost 40x adjusted net profits for that, with massive share-based compensation expenses not even included in that number yet. AVGO surely was a compelling buy in April -- unfortunately, I missed it there, as I bought some other stocks -- but right here, I don't consider it a buy.
